title: 2 Kings 8
subtitle: Hazael, Joram, Ahaziah — The Net Tightens

sections.header: Verses 1–15 — The Shunammite’s Land Restored; Elisha Weeps Over Hazael

heb.paragraph: Elisha weeps (v.11) — not for himself but for the future suffering Hazael will inflict on Israel. The prophet sees what the diplomat will become: “You will set fire to their fortified places, kill their young men, dash their children to the ground, and rip open their pregnant women.” Prophetic sight brings prophetic grief.

panels.ctx: Two scenes intercut: the Shunammite woman from chapter 4 returns from seven years in Philistia and has her land restored — at the exact moment Gehazi is telling the king about Elisha’s miracle of raising her son. Providence arranges the timing. Then Elisha visits Damascus, where Ben-Hadad is ill. Hazael is sent to ask whether the king will recover. Elisha’s answer is deliberately ambiguous: “Go, say to him, ‘You will certainly recover’ — but the LORD has revealed to me that he will in fact die.” Elisha stares at Hazael and weeps: he sees the atrocities Hazael will commit against Israel. The next day Hazael smothers Ben-Hadad with a wet cloth and seizes the throne.

cross.note: At Horeb, God told Elijah to anoint Hazael king of Aram. Elisha now fulfils this commission — the prophetic word from Sinai reaches Damascus.
“For three sins of Damascus, even for four, I will not relent. Because she threshed Gilead with sledges having iron teeth.” Amos condemns the very atrocities Elisha foresees here.

notes.note: Elisha’s tears show that true prophets are not cold dispensers of divine decrees but broken-hearted witnesses to human suffering. The prophet who sees most clearly weeps most deeply.
Hazael’s rise to power through murder warns us that God’s instruments are not always God’s saints. He uses the wicked to accomplish his purposes, then holds them accountable for their wickedness.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The Hebrew is ambiguous: lō-yimût can be read as “he will certainly not die” (from the disease) or “he will certainly die” (by other means). The ketiv-qere variation here suggests the ambiguity is original and deliberate.

wiseman.source: Donald J. Wiseman, 1 and 2 Kings, Tyndale OT Commentary (1993) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: An Assyrian inscription of Shalmaneser III refers to Hazael as “son of nobody” — i.e., a usurper without royal lineage. This confirms the biblical account of Hazael’s seizure of power by violence rather than legitimate succession.

The atrocities Elisha describes — killing young men, dashing children, ripping open pregnant women — are stock language for the horrors of ANE warfare, attested in Assyrian royal inscriptions. The Assyrians boasted of such acts; Israelite prophets wept over them.

provan.source: Iain W. Provan, 1 and 2 Kings, New International Biblical Commentary (1995) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The intercutting of the Shunammite’s restoration with the Hazael scene creates a structural contrast: God’s care for individuals (the woman’s land) versus God’s use of nations as instruments of judgment (Hazael’s rise). Both are acts of sovereignty.

The narrator reports the regicide without moral commentary. The Deuteronomistic historian trusts the reader to supply the judgment: this is what happens when God’s instruments are themselves morally corrupt.

sections.header: Verses 16–29 — Joram and Ahaziah of Judah; the House of Ahab Corrupts Judah

panels.ctx: The focus shifts to Judah. Joram son of Jehoshaphat becomes king — and “walked in the ways of the kings of Israel, as the house of Ahab had done, for he married a daughter of Ahab.” The Omride corruption has crossed the border through marriage alliance. Edom revolts; Judah loses control. Then Ahaziah succeeds and continues the same pattern. The stage is set for Jehu’s revolution: both kingdoms are now ruled by kings connected to the house of Ahab, and both are ripe for judgment.

cross.note: Ahab’s marriage to Jezebel introduced Baal worship. Now the pattern repeats: intermarriage with Ahab’s house imports Ahab’s sins into Judah.
The parallel in Chronicles adds that Joram murdered his brothers upon taking the throne — further evidence of the Omride corruption spreading through the marriage alliance.

notes.note: God preserves Judah not for the sake of its current king but for the sake of his promise to David. This teaches that God’s faithfulness to his covenant outlasts the unfaithfulness of his people.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The “lamp” (nīr) metaphor for the Davidic dynasty appears also in 1 Kgs 11:36 and 15:4. It signifies ongoing dynastic presence — the flame of David’s house has not been extinguished.

wiseman.source: Donald J. Wiseman, 1 and 2 Kings, Tyndale OT Commentary (1993) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Edom’s revolt from Judahite control is confirmed by archaeological evidence from the 9th century BC. The copper mines at Timna and the Red Sea port at Ezion-Geber were significant economic assets; their loss would have been felt severely.

provan.source: Iain W. Provan, 1 and 2 Kings, New International Biblical Commentary (1995) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The lamplight metaphor — “to maintain a lamp for David” — means that the Davidic line continues to burn even when individual kings are faithless. The promise to David is unconditional in its persistence, even though individual kings face consequences.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVThe king asked the woman about it, and she told him. Then he assigned an official to her case and said to him, \u201cGive back everything that belonged to her, including all the income from her land from the day she left the country until now.\u201dESVAnd when the king asked the woman, she told him. So the king appointed an official for her, saying, \u201cRestore all that was hers, together with all the produce of the fields from the day that she left the land until now.\u201dNIVAt the end of the seven years she came back from the land of the Philistines and went to appeal to the king for her house and land.ESVAnd at the end of the seven years, when the woman returned from the land of the Philistines, she went to appeal to the king for her house and her land.
